title: A pre-manufacturing approach for verification of radiological quality of anthropomorphic phantoms
author: Bliznakova, Kristina
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This study proposes an effective and cost-efficient protocol to investigate the suitability of 3D printing materials in the design of anthropomorphic phantoms before their manufacturing. 
 The process starts with the choice of 3D printing filaments to be used in the phantom development. These filaments may be commercial or in-house developed. The in-house developed filaments are produced by mixing the binder (in pellet form) with the filler (in powder form). The final mixture is inserted into the extruder - the filament maker. Once produced, the filament is cut into pellets again and reintroduced into the extruder. This process is repeated multiple times to ensure the final filament achieves a high level of homogeneity. Further, the filaments are grinded with a dental micro-motor in a powdered form and subjected to elemental analysis. The material is analysed with elemental analyser. The obtained elemental composition is introduced in an X-ray imaging computational simulator, capable of reproducing Computed Tomography (CT) numbers. 
 Various 3D printing filaments, including both commercial and in-house varieties, underwent elemental analysis. Simultaneously, samples prepared from these materials were scanned at a CT clinical facility, and Hounsfield Unit (HU) values were measured. Among these materials, HIPS-E and TPC-F, which are used with fused deposition modelling printers, emerged as suitable choices for replicating the breast. The computational anthropomorphic model, featuring segmented breast adipose and glandular tissues, was successfully realized using the combination of HIPS-E and TPC-F. The simulated breast CT slices exhibited remarkable visual and quantitative similarity to the HU of the original breast tissues. 
 The proposed protocol reduces the time required for extensive experimental studies, which typically involve lengthy phantom printing and evaluation at dedicated X-ray facilities.
